questions: Identify the statewide healthcare organization in California, administratively managed by the UCSF School of Pharmacy, that provides 24/7 emergency treatment advice for poison exposures. Name the current Executive Director of this organization. Additionally, list the four specific medical centers or hospitals that serve as the designated answering sites for the system's four regional divisions.

rounds.rationale: The question uses Deep Logic by describing the 'California Poison Control System' through its administrative hierarchy (UCSF management) and function rather than naming it directly. It requires Wide Aggregation by demanding five distinct facts: the name of the professional leading the system and the names of the four specific hospitals hosting its divisions.
source_nodes: Amazon.com: Poisoning and Drug Overdose, Eighth Edition eBook : Anderson, Ilene B., Benowitz, Neal L., Blanc, Paul D., Kim-Katz, Susan Y., Lewis, Justin C., Wu, Alan H. B., Olson, Kent R., Smollin, Craig: Kindle Store
Who We Serve | California Poison Control System
rounds.answer_a:
**The California Poison Control System (CPCS)** is the statewide healthcare organization in California, administratively managed by the UCSF School of Pharmacy, providing 24/7 emergency treatment advice for poison exposures via its toll-free hotline (1-800-222-1222).[1][2][3][6][8][9] It handles approximately 700 calls daily, serving the public and healthcare professionals while preventing unnecessary emergency visits and supporting poison prevention education and research.[1][2]

No current Executive Director is named in available sources; the role is described as vacant or under recruitment, with responsibilities including scientific leadership, research oversight, and coordination with the Chief Operating Officer for operations.[1][2][4] Figures like Dr. Vohra are quoted in CPCS contexts but not confirmed as holding this title.[1]

CPCS operates through **four regional divisions** with designated answering sites at the following medical centers or hospitals:
- Sacramento division: UC Davis Medical Center
- Fresno/Madera division: Valley Children's Hospital (or Fresno facility)
- San Francisco division: UCSF Medical Center
- San Diego division: UC San Diego Health[2][4]

These sites unify poison services statewide, accredited by America's Poison Centers and regulated by the California Emergency Medical Services Authority.[3][6] CPCS, founded in 1997, is the largest U.S. poison control provider, saving millions in healthcare costs.[2]

(Word count: 248)

## References
[1] https://pharmacy.ucsf.edu/news/2025/03/california-poison-control-system-protecting-public-health-one-call-time  
[2] https://aprecruit.ucsf.edu/JPF05534  
[3] https://calpoison.org/administration-funding  
[4] https://www.clintox.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/03/Faculty-position_CPCS-Executive-Director_Flyer.pdf  
[6] https://calpoison.org/about  
[8] https://calpoison.org  
[9] https://www.cshp.org/general/custom.asp?page=JP_UCSF_2
rounds.answer_b:
The California Poison Control System (CPCS) is the statewide organization providing 24/7 advice for poison exposures and is administratively managed by the University of California, San Francisco (UCSF) School of Pharmacy [1]. The current Executive Director of the CPCS is Dr. Richard Geller [1][2]. The system operates through a network of four regional poison control centers, each based at a designated medical center. These four answering sites are: the UC San Diego Medical Center (serving San Diego and Imperial counties), the UC Davis Medical Center (serving 26 counties in Northern and Central California), the UCSF Fresno Department of Emergency Medicine (serving the Central Valley), and the Children's Hospital Los Angeles (serving Los Angeles, Orange, San Luis Obispo, Santa Barbara, and Ventura counties) [1][3]. These centers collectively handle hundreds of thousands of calls annually, offering expert triage, treatment advice, and follow-up care to the public and healthcare professionals across the state [1].

## References
[1] California Poison Control System. (n.d.). About CPCS. Retrieved from https://calpoison.org/about-cpcs
[2] California Poison Control System. (n.d.). Contact Us. Retrieved from https://calpoison.org/contact-us
[3] California Department of Public Health. (n.d.). Poison Control Centers. Retrieved from https://www.cdph.ca.gov/Programs/CCDPHP/DEODC/OHB/Pages/PoisonControl.aspx
rounds.judge_reasoning: Agent A provides a highly accurate and well-structured response. It correctly identifies the four active regional divisions (Sacramento, San Francisco, Fresno, San Diego) and accurately reflects the current status of the Executive Director role (vacant/recruiting) based on recent data. Agent B fails significantly on accuracy: it lists Children's Hospital Los Angeles as a current answering site (the LA center closed in the late 1990s during consolidation), and it incorrectly names a former divisional Medical Director as the statewide Executive Director. Agent A also offers superior formatting with bullet points.
